summary: My name is Akichiyo Mayeda, born on February 1, 1902, in Wakayama Prefecture. My father named me Akichiyo, meaning 'autumn' and 'a thousand years,' hoping I would live a long life. I was the only girl among four brothers and was quite spoilt growing up. In 1920, at age 17, I came to America as a picture bride and faced many hardships, especially living with my strict father-in-law while helping with strawberry farming near Perking, California.

I was born in Wakayama Prefecture, a farming village near Koyasan, not a fishing village. My family lived there before I came to America in 1920. It was a quiet rural place where my father owned a weaving factory before becoming a farmer. The people there were different from those in fishing villages, who were known to be rough and hot-tempered.

Our village in Wakayama Prefecture was near Koyasan, a place known for its temples. My father was once a teacher at a temple school there, teaching people to read and write. Growing up near Koyasan influenced my family life, especially with my father's connection to the temple and his wearing of hakama when I was little.

When I arrived in America in January 1920, we first landed in San Francisco. My father-in-law welcomed me there with a suitcase full of clothes and helped dress me in traditional attire, including a corset and dress. It was my first impression of America, and I was surprised by the differences from the pictures I had seen before coming.

After arriving in San Francisco, we traveled to Sacramento, California, and from there took a taxi to our home near Perking. Sacramento was a stopover on the way to the strawberry farm where my husband worked. It was part of my early days in America, adjusting to a new life far from Japan.

My husband was growing strawberries near Perking, California, where about 40 to 50 Japanese farmers worked together. Our home was a small black house, quite humble compared to the white house I first thought was ours. Life there was hard, especially with my strict father-in-law and the demanding farm work during the busy season.

Three years after I arrived in America, a prosperous time came following World War I. This economic upturn helped my father-in-law make some profit from the farm. It was during this period that my mother-in-law took my father-in-law back to Japan, which improved my life significantly.

I came to America as a Japanese picture bride in 1920 when I was 17 years old. My marriage was arranged by my parents without my consent, and I had never been to America before. I had imagined America from pictures showing beautiful brick houses, but the reality was very different and much harder than I expected.
